item.title: Publishing my first R package on CRAN
item.description:
As an R user for the last 10 years or so, I always thought it would be very cool to contribute to the great universe of packages available but I was never sure what I could contribute with… This was true until recently, when I started developing some code to extract data from a Google API. Going through the list of available R packages, I couldn’t find another package doing exactly what I needed so I thought that perhaps I could develop the kind of software I was looking for. A few weeks gone by, I can now count myself among the many contributors of R packages available on CRAN!

gkgraphR

The gkgraphR package provides a simple means to interact with the Google Knowledge Graph API. This API allows users to query the Google Knowledge Graph for entities recognized by Google and extract their associated information, including IDs and tags for example. It allows users to carry out keyword-based queries or ID-based queries. This former is useful when users are interested in finding topics recognized by Google that match a given term whereas the latter is perhaps most useful if a list of topic IDs is available but the user requires further details on each topic. The package allows users to tweak all the available APi parameters and return results in several formats, ranging from a standard data.frame to the original JSON API response. If you are interested, you can find more details about the gkgraphR package here.

Next steps

The purpose of this package was to provide a simple but interactive means of access to the Google Knowledge Graph API and I think it achieves this goal throught a simple function. Unless there are upcoming changes to the API, I don’t foresse any further work on this package anytime soon. It may be possible to include access to other knowledge graphs in the future, but this is not in my plans in the near future. On the other hand, this experience thought me much about code development and I have a few other ideas that I may consider developing code for to share with the broader R community. Stay tuned! ;)

item.title: Taxonomic bias in amphibian research: Are researchers responding to conservation need?
Hello world!
item.description:
Welcome to my personal website!

Who am I?

I am an enthusiastic conservation researcher working for the preservation of our natural world!

Why a personal website?

It has been a while since I have been looking for a space where I can share some of my (mis)adventures as a conservation scientist working with large data sets and digital data sources to generate insights that can contribute biodiversity conservation. Here, I hope to share of my (wild) thoughts at the intersection between the natural and the digital worlds. Keep an eye on this space and feel free to follow me on Twitter to stay up to date with the latest news!

item.title: iEcology: Harnessing Large Online Resources to Generate Ecological Insights
Societal attention toward extinction threats: a comparison between climate change and biological invasions
The ghosts of forests past and future: deforestation and botanical sampling in the Brazilian Amazon
Integrating long-term ecological research (LTER) and marine protected area management: Challenges and solutions
Increasing biodiversity in wood-pastures by protecting small shrubby patches
Monitoring and mapping non-governmental conservation action in Amazonia
Drivers of taxonomic bias in conservation research: a global analysis of terrestrial mammals
Male post‐breeding movements and stopover habitat selection of an endangered short‐distance migrant, the Little Bustard Tetrax tetrax
The role of species charisma in biological invasions
Developing and validating a nestling photographic aging guide for cavity-nesting birds: an example with the European Bee-eater (Merops apiaster)
A culturomics approach to quantifying the salience of species on the global internet
Assets em Áreas Protegidas - Estudo de caso em Áreas Úmidas (in English: Protected area assets - A case study with wetlands)
Are Protected Areas undervalued? An asset-based analysis of Brazilian Protected Area Management Plans
Using ignorance scores to explore biodiversity recording effort for multiple taxa in the Caatinga
Scientific Productivity of Brazilian Ecological Stations
Known unknowns: Filling the gaps in scientific knowledge production in the Caatinga
Assessing cultural ecosystem services of a large marine protected area through social media photographs
Data mining on YouTube reveals fisher group-specific harvesting patterns and social engagement in recreational anglers and spearfishers
Inferring public interest from search engine data requires caution
An example preprint / working paper
item.description:
Click the Slides button above to demo Academic’s Markdown slides feature.

Supplementary notes can be added here, including code and math.

item.title: Hunting in Brazil: What are the options?
Display Jupyter Notebooks with Academic
item.description:
from IPython.core.display import Image
Image('https://www.python.org/static/community_logos/python-logo-master-v3-TM-flattened.png')


print("Welcome to Academic!")

Welcome to Academic!

Install Python and JupyterLab

Install Anaconda which includes Python 3 and JupyterLab.

Alternatively, install JupyterLab with pip3 install jupyterlab.

Create or upload a Jupyter notebook

Run the following commands in your Terminal, substituting <MY-WEBSITE-FOLDER> and <SHORT-POST-TITLE> with the file path to your Academic website folder and a short title for your blog post (use hyphens instead of spaces), respectively:

mkdir -p <MY-WEBSITE-FOLDER>/content/post/<SHORT-POST-TITLE>/
cd <MY-WEBSITE-FOLDER>/content/post/<SHORT-POST-TITLE>/
jupyter lab index.ipynb

The jupyter command above will launch the JupyterLab editor, allowing us to add Academic metadata and write the content.

Edit your post metadata

The first cell of your Jupter notebook will contain your post metadata ( front matter).

In Jupter, choose Markdown as the type of the first cell and wrap your Academic metadata in three dashes, indicating that it is YAML front matter:

---
title: My post's title
date: 2019-09-01

# Put any other Academic metadata here...
---

Edit the metadata of your post, using the documentation as a guide to the available options.

To set a featured image, place an image named featured into your post’s folder.

For other tips, such as using math, see the guide on writing content with Academic.

Convert notebook to Markdown

jupyter nbconvert index.ipynb --to markdown --NbConvertApp.output_files_dir=.

Example

This post was created with Jupyter. The orginal files can be found at https://github.com/gcushen/hugo-academic/tree/master/exampleSite/content/post/jupyter

Google trends data need validation - Comment on Durmuşoğlu (2017)
On the overlap between scientific and societal taxonomic attentions - Insights for conservation
Jocelyn Thorpe, Stephanie Rutherford and L. Anders Sandberg (eds), Methodological Challenges in Nature-Culture and Environmental History Research
Pivotal 20th century contributions to the development of the Anthropocene concept - overview and implications
A salience index for integrating multiple user perspectives in cultural ecosystem service assessments
Nomenclature instability in species culturomic assessments: Why synonyms matter
Using machine learning to disentangle homonyms in large text corpora
Culturomic assessment of Brazilian protected areas: Exploring a novel index of protected area visibility
Contribution of spatially explicit models to climate change adaptation and mitigation plans for a priority forest habitat
EU protected area network did not prevent a country wide population decline in a threatened grassland bird
Are capacity deficits in local government leaving the Amazon vulnerable to environmental change?
Cultural Services in the Caatinga
Understanding non-compliance: Local people’s perceptions of natural resource exploitation inside two national parks in northeast Brazil
Drier climate shifts leaf morphology in Amazonian trees
Protected areas buffer the Brazilian semi‐arid biome from climate change
Protected area asset stewardship
The power and the promise of culturomics
Internet scientific name frequency as an indicator of cultural salience of biodiversity
A Bibliometric Analysis of High Impact Research in the Middle East Using Champion Works
Measuring what matters – Identifying indicators of success for Brazilian marine protected areas
Conservation Culturomics
Post‐release monitoring of Antillean manatees: an assessment of the Brazilian rehabilitation and release programme
Academic: the website builder for Hugo
item.description:
Create a free website with Academic using Markdown, Jupyter, or RStudio. Choose a beautiful color theme and build anything with the Page Builder - over 40 widgets, themes, and language packs included!

Check out the latest demo of what you’ll get in less than 10 minutes, or view the showcase of personal, project, and business sites.

  • 👉 Get Started
  • 📚 View the documentation
  • 💬 Ask a question on the forum
  • 👥 Chat with the community
  • 🐦 Twitter: @source_themes @GeorgeCushen #MadeWithAcademic
  • 💡 Request a feature or report a bug
  • ⬆️ Updating? View the Update Guide and Release Notes
  • ❤️ Support development of Academic:
    • ☕️ Donate a coffee
    • 💵 Become a backer on Patreon
    • 🖼️ Decorate your laptop or journal with an Academic sticker
    • 👕 Wear the T-shirt
    • 👩‍💻 Contribute

Key features:

  • Page builder - Create anything with widgets and elements
  • Edit any type of content - Blog posts, publications, talks, slides, projects, and more!
  • Create content in Markdown, Jupyter, or RStudio
  • Plugin System - Fully customizable color and font themes
  • Display Code and Math - Code highlighting and LaTeX math supported
  • Integrations - Google Analytics, Disqus commenting, Maps, Contact Forms, and more!
  • Beautiful Site - Simple and refreshing one page design
  • Industry-Leading SEO - Help get your website found on search engines and social media
  • Media Galleries - Display your images and videos with captions in a customizable gallery
  • Mobile Friendly - Look amazing on every screen with a mobile friendly version of your site
  • Multi-language - 15+ language packs including English, 中文, and Português
  • Multi-user - Each author gets their own profile page
  • Privacy Pack - Assists with GDPR
  • Stand Out - Bring your site to life with animation, parallax backgrounds, and scroll effects
  • One-Click Deployment - No servers. No databases. Only files.

Themes

Academic comes with automatic day (light) and night (dark) mode built-in. Alternatively, visitors can choose their preferred mode - click the sun/moon icon in the top right of the Demo to see it in action! Day/night mode can also be disabled by the site admin in params.toml.

Choose a stunning theme and font for your site. Themes are fully customizable.

Ecosystem

  • Academic Admin: An admin tool to import publications from BibTeX or import assets for an offline site
  • Academic Scripts: Scripts to help migrate content to new versions of Academic

Install

You can choose from one of the following four methods to install:

  • one-click install using your web browser (recommended)
  • install on your computer using Git with the Command Prompt/Terminal app
  • install on your computer by downloading the ZIP files
  • install on your computer with RStudio

Then personalize and deploy your new site.

Updating

View the Update Guide.

Feel free to star the project on Github to help keep track of updates.

License

Copyright 2016-present George Cushen.

Released under the MIT license.
